card.title: How Fossils Actually Form
card.teaser: Almost everything that's ever lived has vanished without a trace. Fossils are the lucky few — and even those are mostly rock, not bone.
card.coverImagePrompt: A cracked slab of sandstone split open on a desert floor revealing a perfectly preserved ammonite spiral, golden-hour light, cinematic field photography

lessons.title: The lottery of preservation
lessons.body: Paleontologists estimate that fewer than one in a million animals ever becomes a fossil. The rest are eaten, weathered, scattered, or decomposed to nothing. Every fossil you've seen in a museum is a winner of a very long, very cold lottery.

visual.imagePrompt: A vast open riverbank scattered with countless tiny white bone fragments disappearing into mud, only one intact skeleton visible, wide cinematic shot

lessons.title: Step one: rapid burial
lessons.body: Fossilization almost always starts with a lucky burial. An animal dies in just the right place — the silt of a river delta, a volcanic ash fall, a tar pit, a beach under a storm. Sediment covers the body fast enough that scavengers, oxygen, and weather can't finish it off.

keyTerms.definition: Loose particles of mud, sand, or ash that settle and accumulate in layers
The study of what happens to an organism between death and fossilization

visual.imagePrompt: Cross-section diagram of a dinosaur carcass buried under river sediment layers, labeled stages of burial, scientific illustration

lessons.title: Step two: stone by stone
lessons.body: Once buried, soft tissues usually rot away, leaving bones, teeth, or shells. Over thousands of years, groundwater loaded with minerals — silica, calcite, pyrite — seeps into the tiny pores in the bone. Molecule by molecule, the original material is replaced by rock in a process called permineralization. What you hold in your hand isn't really bone anymore. It's a stone copy, down to the cell shape.

visual.imagePrompt: Microscopic view of mineral-rich groundwater infiltrating bone cell structures, glowing crystals forming inside pores, technical illustration

lessons.title: Not just bones
lessons.body: Fossils can preserve a surprising amount. Trackways record the gait of animals dead 200 million years. Coprolites — fossilized dung — reveal diet. In Liaoning, China, volcanic ash has preserved dinosaur feathers, skin pigments, and even stomach contents. In rare cases, researchers have extracted protein fragments from 80-million-year-old bones.
lessons.funFact: The colour of some dinosaur feathers has been reconstructed by finding fossilized melanosomes — the same pigment structures that give modern birds their hues.

visual.imagePrompt: A fossilized dinosaur footprint in cracked red-brown mudstone beside a modern human boot for scale, warm desert lighting

lessons.title: A filtered window
lessons.body: The fossil record is beautiful but biased. Hard-shelled marine creatures turn up often; soft-bodied forest dwellers almost never. When you stare at a museum dinosaur, remember you're seeing the rare survivors of an immense, almost-silent biography of life on Earth.

quiz.question: Roughly what fraction of ancient animals ever become fossils?
quiz.explanation: Fossilization requires such a specific sequence of events that fewer than one in a million creatures ever leaves a trace.

quiz.question: Why is rapid burial so important for fossilization?
options: It shields the body from scavengers, oxygen, and weathering
It forces the tissue to petrify immediately
It compresses the bones into rock
quiz.explanation: Burial protects the remains long enough for the slow chemistry of mineralization to take over.

quiz.question: In permineralization, what happens to the original bone?
options: It's slowly replaced by minerals in a stone copy
It shrinks to half its size
quiz.explanation: Mineral-laden water fills the pores and replaces organic material, producing a rock replica with fine detail preserved.

sources.title: Reconstruction of the original melanin colors in a 300-million-year-old pigmented soft tissue
